data.title: The Vita Coco Company: Still On Hold Until The Trade Situation Uncertainty Is Over
data.description:
Summary

I gave a neutral rating to The Vita Coco Company (NASDAQ:COCO) previously, as I was worried about the tariff situation, which, if left unchanged, could cause COCO to miss consensus estimates. 2Q25 results were strong, particularly in core coconut water, which posted 25% y/y growth. Momentum remains solid both in the US and internationally, and management raised FY25 guidance. However, gross margin saw a sharp decline due to tariffs, and further escalation remains a risk. With that, I believe the story is improving, but not enough to justify turning bullish until tariff clarity emerges.

2Q25 Earnings Review

COCO delivered another impressive quarter, with 2Q25 net sales growing 17.1% y/y to $168.8 million. The key driver once again was the core Vita Coco Coconut Water brand, which saw a remarkable 25% y/y increase in global net sales. This strength was broad-based, with the Americas segment growing 14% y/y to $142 million and International growing 36.6% y/y to $26.8 million. However, as I flagged in my last update, the near-term setup is not without noise. The strong performance was partially offset by a 25.2% y/y drop in private label sales, primarily due to the loss of certain service regions. While this business is less strategic, the decline still acts as a drag on consolidated growth. The bigger concern, though, lies in the cost structure. Gross margin fell sharply by ~450 bps to 36.3%, due to a combination of elevated ocean freight, higher finished goods costs, and the early impact of the new 10% US import tariffs. This margin compression was apparent in the P&L, with adj. EBITDA fell to $29.2 million from $32.2 million a year ago, and the EBITDA margin narrowed to 17.3%.

Given the strong performance, management raised its FY25 net sales guidance to a new range of $565–$580 million, implying low-teens full-year growth. However, there was some conservatism. The updated guide suggests growth will slow to roughly 5% in 2H25, a sharp deceleration from the ~17% pace seen so far. In my view, this is a prudent move to protect against downside risk (could be due to macro volatility or tariff-driven cost inflation).

Fundamental Health Still Good

The broader demand landscape remains very healthy, as coconut water is proving to be one of the strongest segments in the beverage aisle right now. According to scanner data (shown in the earnings slide deck), US coconut water volumes grew 12% y/y in the last 13 weeks, and notably, this growth is being driven by volume—not pricing. That matters because it shows a real uptick in consumer adoption, not just inflation that masks flat demand. COCO is still clearly the leader here, holding a dominant 44.5% US market share. The growth outlook also remains healthy, with management expecting the US market to double over time, and given these current trends (double-digit growth), I don’t think that assumption is implausible. As an equity investor, this growing category narrative also provides some comfort that COCO can continue to sustain this top-line strength despite the macro volatility.

What is also equally encouraging is how the international business is starting to see success. In 2Q25, international net sales grew 36.6% y/y, led by a 43% growth in coconut water sales in Europe. Even in the UK, the coconut water category grew by 35% on a year-to-date basis (while COCO grew 39% over the same period). This shows that COCO's years of investments in core markets like the UK and Germany are now paying off, and the interesting aspect here, which makes this momentum feel more sustainable, is that COCO is growing not just by stealing share but also by growing alongside the entire category. In other words, the growth runway is a lot longer. Management believes the European business could eventually reach the same scale as the Americas segment today, and while that’s a long road, if true, the upside is huge, as international is just 15% of total sales.

Improving Walmart Outlook

The Walmart (WMT) overhang I previously discussed looks like it’s beginning to fade. While still a drag, management noted that the y/y sales decline at WMT has moderated to the high-single- or low-double-digit range, which is better than the earlier part of 2025. More importantly, early talks around the 2026 shelf reset have been encouraging. If that plays out positively, WMT could shift from being a headwind to a growth driver. In my view, this is a high-impact near-term catalyst, especially given the channel’s volume potential. In the interim, performance on the shelf has actually been strong. Velocity metrics show that sales per point of distribution are up >50%, which highlights that consumer demand is not the issue, distribution gaps are, and that is a fixable problem if discussions go well.

Tariff Risks Are Now Front and Center

The tariff situation has gone from an uncertain risk to a real drag. The 10% baseline US import tariff is now fully embedded into the FY25 guide, and it’s already showing up in the numbers (2Q25 gross margin contracted by 450 bps, largely due to this). What makes things more complicated is what the guide doesn’t include: the impact from additional tariffs that have been proposed but not yet implemented. That makes the forward earnings outlook harder to underwrite with confidence.

To be clear, COCO is not doing nothing. They’ve reiterated their long-term plans to offset tariff-related pressures through pricing and sourcing adjustments, and the supply chain is reasonably diversified. But in the near term, I don’t see how COCO can completely escape from this tariff situation. The fact of the matter is tariffs hit P&L immediately, while mitigation takes time to roll through. That leaves a window of vulnerability, especially if new tariffs are introduced with short notice. One instance here is the 50% tariff on Brazil, which was not included in the guidance.

Until there’s greater visibility on US trade policy, I think this will continue to act as a cap on valuation. Even with strong brand momentum, investors may hesitate to price in a steep earnings ramp if gross margins remain exposed to tariff shocks.

Valuation

COCO did well at the topline, and the market share metrics are healthy; even the category growth outlook is promising. Like I said before, the uncertainty with tariffs is a huge overhang on the stock sentiment, and clearly, this has been the case. Valuation has trended downwards to 25x forward PE from 28x in May. Just based on the fundamentals (mid- to long-term focus), I think a reversion to the mean is very possible; the question is timing. If the tariff situation gets worse, near-term earnings will continue to see pressure, and that may push valuation even further down. Should readers invest today? If only you had enough holding power to ride through this volatility. If not, I think it makes sense to continue staying neutral until we get clarity on the US trade policy.

Conclusion

I maintain my neutral rating on COCO. There’s no denying that topline momentum is impressive and that category fundamentals are healthy. However, the sharp margin compression and risk of more tariffs introduce too much uncertainty around earnings. Until we get clarity on US trade policy, or at least see evidence that mitigation strategies are working, I think COCO’s upside is capped.

data.title: Mosaic: A Strong Foundation, But Not A Buy (For Now)
data.description:
Introduction and Financials

The Mosaic Company (NYSE:MOS) is an American fertilizer giant with international distribution networks, being the largest US producer of potash and phosphate fertilizer. With solid tailwinds for its commodities, improved production, and better costs, Mosaic is in an interesting position today, being ready to enjoy a solid future for fertilizers despite the natural commodity volatility.

Regarding the margins, we can see that all of them dropped compared to the 5-year averages. However, keep in mind that the past 5 years have been some of the best years for fertilizers we've ever seen, as a result of the Russia-Ukraine war hurting the supply-demand balance significantly, as I will mention below.

As we can expect, the free cash flow has been very volatile throughout the cycle, but it's worth mentioning that the unlevered one has always been positive, which is not bad if we think about the lows we've seen in most commodities back in 2015-2017. The CAPEX, however, is also very significant, as the company has been investing significant amounts of money to develop its production, including the K3 mine in Canada, which is the largest potash mine on the planet. This CAPEX should decrease in the near future, as the company targets some cost reductions on top of a higher production in phosphate this year (recently announced a cut in this guidance), so we could expect better cash flows now than in 2024, especially if we consider the increase in fertilizer prices seen since April, thanks especially to tariffs.

In their Q2 report, they also raised their cost reduction and value capture targets to $250 million, already achieving the $150 million target. This comes after they managed to identify some additional opportunities in automation of administrative functions, supply chain optimization, fixed cost absorption and other cost reductions - to be achieved by the end of 2026.

Financially, based on their Q2 earnings report, we can see a good position, with the current assets covering the current liabilities and a manageable amount of debt. There's $12.58 billion in equity on a ~$10.43 billion market cap after falling on earnings day, which means that they are trading at a PB of slightly below 1 today, which is definitely not bad.

The debt is spread over the long term, with 2027 and 2028 representing the bigger maturities that they'll likely have to refinance or repay to some degree. They should have enough time to do both, and refinancing could be great, especially since I believe we'll see rate cuts coming before that, resulting in potentially lower interest expenses than the $230 million recorded last year.

Dividends and Buybacks

The dividend, like most things about this company, will depend on commodity prices. Right now, the yield is at ~2.4%, representing about $270 million, meaning a payout of around 62.5% based on last year's cash flows, but much less for the TTM level. I believe it's possible to see them increase this dividend in the near future, as the cash flow would allow it.

The company also did buybacks - unfortunately, at all-time highs back in 2022-2023 when they had the money - but have now reduced the repurchases significantly. This is very common for commodity stocks, and I believe it's almost always a bad decision, as companies sometimes spend enough to cover significant amounts of their debt (Mosaic included) at times when the stock is much more expensive than average. So, instead of improving the company's long-term significantly, they just go for whatever pleases the shareholders in the short-term, which is why I always call this practice out when I get the chance.

Risks and Opportunities

However, the most important thing to remember is that, as I mentioned in the Deere & Company (DE) analysis, for example, this is all about the commodity cycle. The potash/fertilizer/etc. market faced significant disruptions, high prices, and high investments for a while now, but as projects such as BHP's (BHP) Jansen become operational in the near future (delayed to mid-2027 now) and the supply-demand balance recovers, we are looking at a normalization in potash prices as well. In the end, this market will be tied to the economy, and although food/agriculture are defensive industries, it doesn't mean there's no volatility in fertilizers - quite the contrary. Since China, India, Brazil, and the US are the main sources of phosphate and potash demand, any improvement in these countries - such as China's policy shift announced at the end of 2024 - should be great for the potash market going forward.

However, as I mentioned in the CF Industries (CF) stock analysis, fertilizers should be more and more required as the land would need more nutrients over time as a result of relatively inefficient planting cycles and agriculture techniques. As CF Industries says, ~50% of the world's food production is already dependent on fertilizer, and this might only continue to grow in the future. Especially in countries such as the US, we have corn as the perfect example, which is excessively subsidized, encouraging inefficient farming practices and little plant rotation, which then depletes the land of its naturally present nutrients, "forcing" farmers to use more and more fertilizer if they want to keep the current production yields. This can also lead to long-term damage to the soil and even turning it barren (at least for corn), and might eventually change if politics get in the mix and lower the subsidies for corn, allowing for a more natural planting cycle and diversity that wouldn't deplete naturally present nutrients and allow pest/disease buildup.

However, another potential disruption could come from the Israel-Iran conflict escalating again in the future. Israel is a key producer of low-cost potash through state-owned companies like ICL (ICL) who also produces about a third of the world's bromine at the same time. The country represents about 5-6% of the world's potash, so affecting their production and supply chains can significantly affect the global markets.

For reference, Russia is the 2nd largest mineral fertilizer exporter after China, with about 18% of the total share of potash fertilizers exports. So, disruptions in Israel should have a much smaller effect and we will likely not see the same prices as we've seen in recent years, but it's still a factor to consider. In this regard, an end to the Russia-Ukraine war - which Trump is lately pushing for more than ever - could actually be bad for the price of fertilizers, basically doing the opposite of what they did when the war started. Still, that's something for the very long-term, when production in these two countries recovers and trade with the EU becomes more like it was before - which can definitely take a while.

As a parenthesis, Europe's exports of Russian fertilizer were relatively stable since the war began (fell about 10% to around 13% of Europe's consumption). However, back in July 2025, we saw a new proposal that introduced a 15% customs duty increase on previously unsanctioned Russian agricultural imports, and eight member states have already stopped Russian fertilizer imports - which could be an opportunity for companies like Mosaic:

For fertilizers, in particular nitrogen fertilizers, the tariff increase will be gradual over the next three years. As of 1 July, an additional tariff of 40-45 euros per tonne will be introduced on most nitrogen fertilizers, such as urea and ammonium nitrate. In addition, if the established import limits are exceeded (2.7 million tons in MY 2025/26, 1.8 million tons in MY 2026/27 and 0.9 million tons in MY 2027/28), maximum protective duties may be applied. In 2024, the EU imported 6.2 million tons of Russian fertilizers, which is a quarter of all fertilizer imports."

Still, as a potential short/medium-term opportunity, we should see rate cuts - despite the recent decision from the Fed to postpone them in July and gave no hints for a September cut - that could potentially lead to more economic activity in the US. High rates are meant to combat inflation by limiting economic activity, while rate cuts lower the cost of borrowing, increase discretionary budgets, make it easier for companies (especially in agriculture) to expand/renew equipment, etc. - an environment that should be quite beneficial for companies like Mosaic and their clients.

As for the very long-term, we have the classic argument of "people will still eat food" - which is a valid summary of the slow and steady growth in potash demand we could expect from a fundamental point of view. A 5.3% demand CAGR should still be very solid, so if we ignore the commodity's volatility and focus only on very long-term tailwinds, potash companies are likely good investments.

However, we can't ignore the commodity cycle. Just because we'll still eat food it doesn't mean we can just buy commodity stocks no matter the price. Sure, Mosaic is a good cash flow producer, but buying at the peak of 2022 when the price was more than double the current one is not exactly a buying moment for commodity stocks, in my opinion. Now however, with prices similar to the bottoms from 2015-2017 seen recently, we might be looking at a very different story.

Also, as Mosaic has significant production in Brazil, we're talking about specific risks that the market generally accounts for, resulting in lower valuations. This is predominantly about the political and economic environment, and the fact that Brazil's interest rates are even at 15% now, which has significant effects on the investments in the country. Cutting them as well could also be an opportunity, encouraging more farming and investments in the agriculture business - which is crucial for Brazil - which can be great for Mosaic as a local producer. This can also help Mosaic avoid Trump's tariffs to some degree, the company being able to offer local production for their markets - although they will still have an impact, of course. They also talk about the relatively weak US grower returns, while globally, especially in Indian rice and Brazil corn/soybean, we can see much better numbers.

As they mention, supplemental government support could be very rewarding in this aspect, which wouldn't be impossible to see from the Trump administration. In fact, Trump's "Big Beautiful Bill" focuses on support and tax cuts for farmers and commodity growers, while also prioritizing "America First" policies. In fact, even during his previous term, we've seen Trump implement some ad-hoc bailout programs for farmers to protect him from the trade war with China, which resulted in billions of dollars in payments to farmers.

Also, as I mentioned in the Intrepid Potash (IPI) analysis, I believe there could be potential to see one of the larger players (Mosaic included) look into buying IPI. This would solidify any player's American exposure, which can be very important in case the tariff situation escalates, while acquiring a solid player at a relatively cheap price compared to peers.

Peer Comparison

Compared to peers such as Nutrien (NTR), ICL Group, CF Industries, and Intrepid Potash - 3 of which I've analyzed in recent articles - we can see Mosaic trading at interesting valuation metrics. For the largest US producer of potash and phosphate fertilizer, trading at lower ratios even than Intrepid Potash (which I believe is relatively attractive now), Mosaic is definitely in an interesting position. Still, the Brazilian assets, which represented about 38.6% of Mosaic's revenue last year, definitely have an impact on their valuation, as these assets tend to be much cheaper compared to American/other peers.

Valuation

For Mosaic's DCF valuation, I'm using a starting FCF of $600 million in 2025 to account for higher production, better prices, and a reduction in CAPEX. This FCF is projected to grow at a 5% CAGR for the first 5 years thanks to the solid fertilizer demand and potential improvements in the global economy leading to higher demand, followed by a 3% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years. Assuming a 2% terminal growth rate and a WACC of 7% - in line with market estimates - and accounting for $4.1 billion in net debt along with 317.23 million shares outstanding, the we get to an enterprise value of ~$13.79 billion, and by subtracting the net debt, we get to an equity value of ~$9.69 billion, giving an intrinsic value of about $30.55 per share, which is very close to the current level.

Even if we assume a higher FCF - say $800 million as a result of even better fertilizer prices - we're looking at a price of $45.02, which would barely be a significant enough profit and margin of safety to confidently call this a Buy.

Conclusions

Overall, given their financials and how much money Mosaic can make, I don't think this price is attractive enough to justify buying, which is why I rate it a Hold, as fertilizers remain solid commodities overall. The yield is not really that attractive compared to their peers, and the overall opportunity doesn't seem that attractive. Sure, Mosaic will do better if their commodities improve, but so will their peers, some of which I believe offer better opportunities.

data.title: Mairs & Power Balanced Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Overview

The stock market entered bear territory in early April (20% below its peak), only to rise 25% from that low by the end of the quarter, exemplifying an unsettled environment. Tariff uncertainty, conflicts in the Middle East, rising national debt, and the worst start to a year for the dollar since 1973, have created volatility that led the markets right back to where they started at the beginning of the year.

Looking at the indices for the second quarter, the S&P 500 Total Return (TR) was 10.94% for the quarter and is 6.20% for the year. Meanwhile, the Russell 2000 (TR) was 8.50% in the quarter and is -1.79% year-to-date, and the Bloomberg U.S. Government/Credit Bond Index was 1.22% in the second quarter and is 3.95% so far in 2025. Growth stocks once again took a leadership position, particularly in Information Technology and Communication Services, while Energy and Health Care lagged. In all, nine of 11 sectors finished the quarter in positive territory.

Despite ongoing hectic headlines, the economic backdrop has remained relatively stable. The pace of inflation continues to decelerate, with the Consumer Price Index (CPI) rising just 2.4% year-over-year in June, down from 3.1% in February. Additionally, the ISM manufacturing and services surveys both improved in recent months, though manufacturing remains in contractionary territory. On the consumer front, the University of Michigan’s Index of Consumer Sentiment rebounded for the first time in six months as expectations improved around tariffs and inflation.

On the negative side, the job market is showing some softening. As of the June ADP National Employment Report, private sector employment declined by 33,000 jobs, marking the first monthly drop in more than two years. While the Labor Department’s employment report showed unexpectedly positive job growth and a lower unemployment rate, nearly half of the increases in nonfarm payrolls came from the government sector, suggesting private employers are pulling back on hiring due to an ongoing uncertain environment.

After contracting in the first quarter of the year, equity market valuation levels expanded back into above-average territory during the second quarter. Currently, the forward 12-month price-to-earnings ratio for the S&P 500 is 22 times earnings, above its five- and 10-year average. However, as has been the case for a while now, the S&P 600 Small Cap Index P/E is 15 times earnings, which is below both its 5- and 10-year average, indicating a persistent undervaluation of smaller companies in the current environment.

Future Outlook

The mixed economic data has led the Federal Reserve (Fed) to maintain its wait-and-see approach, holding interest rates steady as it monitors the potential impact from tariffs, which remain a wildcard. Yet, with inflation pressure easing and the labor market cooling, the likelihood of additional Fed rate cuts in the second half of the year appears to be increasing.

A key support for the recent stock market rally has been the resiliency of corporate earnings, which have been remarkably stable despite the unsettled macro environment. Analysts expect 9% growth for the full year, a reduction from the 13% projected at the beginning of 2025 but an indication that companies are navigating the volatile environment with minimal disruption so far. However, trade uncertainty is likely to remain, and we expect more twists and turns ahead.

Beyond tariffs, inflation, and interest rates, the fervor around artificial intelligence came roaring back in the second quarter. We expect this to continue as the technology and investment surrounding AI, automation, and robotics are moving at breakneck speed. We’re seeing impacts and upgrades within a year, if not a few months.

We believe these technological innovations promise to reshape not just markets, but the way businesses operate. We also believe these are the early days of disruption to entire industries, companies, and the economy. Regardless of how far along those innovations are and what’s yet to come, the pace of technological advancement in recent months is nothing short of extraordinary. We remain positive on the long-term prospects for AI, automation, and robotics. From logistics to healthcare to software development, companies are already realizing gains in productivity and efficiency. Still, we are focused on our valuation discipline to find opportunities in the market. We continue to seek companies with durable competitive advantages and especially those with the scale and wisdom to sacrifice short-term profits for long-term investments in the technological advancements needed to stay ahead of their competitors.

Performance Review

The Mairs & Power Balanced Fund finished the first half of 2025 up 2.66%. The Fund underperformed both the benchmark composite index (60% S&P 500 Total Return Index and 40% Bloomberg U.S. Government/Credit Bond Index), which was up 5.43%, and the Morningstar Moderate Allocation peer group, which rose 5.67%.

Performance data quoted represents past performance and does not guarantee future results. The investment return and principal value of an investment will fluctuate so that an investor’s shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance of the Fund may be lower or higher than the performance quoted. For the most recent month-end performance figures, please call Shareholder Services at (800) 304-7404. Expense Ratio 0.71%.

The Fund’s overweight to equities generally dragged on performance due to the selection of securities during the first half, which impacted performance meaningfully. The bonds in the Balanced Fund had positive mid-single-digit total returns.

The Fund’s overweight to the Health Care sector, combined with its exposure to specific names in the sector, negatively impacted relative performance during the first half of 2025. UnitedHealth had a surprising decline in the first six months of 2025 as the company mispriced its business and saw an abrupt management change. The company is likely to fix its pricing issues for new members, while it has some work to improve its Medicare Advantage managed care business. In mid-May, the company’s CEO left rather abruptly while the previous CEO, Stephen Hemsley, is leaving retirement to “steady the ship.” It also suspended its earnings guidance, creating unease among market participants. The company still has an enviable earnings profile and historically has proven its ability to manage through temporary disturbances. We are holding our UnitedHealth position in the Balanced Fund and have not adjusted the Fund’s position in the company since the most recent set of news. Additionally, BioTechne (TECH), which makes high quality ingredients for biopharmaceutical research and production, underperformed due to uncertainty around the industry’s funding environment given recent federal funding cuts and a weak venture capital funding environment.

We have taken advantage of the recent weakness to add to our Bio-Techne holdings. Offsetting these, Abbott Laboratories (ABT) outperformed as organic results improved driven by its medical devices business, specifically its core diabetes business.

The Fund’s Industrials sector overweight aided results in the first half of 2025, but selection within the sector more than offset this, resulting in an overall negative relative impact for the Fund. Toro (TTC), where the Fund has a meaningful position, reported slowing revenue growth on weaker consumer sales, and the company lowered its guidance, leading the stock to fall. Offsetting this slightly was Fastenal (FAST), whose continued growth suggested market share gains as the company faces similar end market weakness on slowing industrial demand.

The Fund’s overweight to the Financials sector aided performance in the first half of 2025 but was also offset by stock selection within the sector. Fiserv had a difficult first six months of 2025 as the company revised its guidance lower for its Clover product, a cloud-based payment platform, which is at the core of its growth in merchant acceptance. The company has performed well in recent years, and the Clover product has been a meaningful part of that growth. Although we remain confident in the company’s future, the downward revision of expectations had an outsized impact on stock performance. Offsetting this, JPMorgan & Chase (JPM), Wells Fargo (WFC), and Visa (V) all outperformed as the expectation of a recession has diminished and a trend toward deregulation should benefit the banking and payments sectors. Wells Fargo further benefited as several consent decrees, which outline specific reforms and improvements, were lifted by various regulators. The most impactful of these was the Fed’s restriction on growth, which was unprecedented and lasted seven years. With these restrictions being largely lifted, the company is now in a much better position for future growth. Separately, as evidence mounts of a declining durable competitive advantage at what has been a long-term core holding in U.S. Bancorp, the Fund meaningfully trimmed its position in the bank.

The Fund built a new position in WEC Energy during the first half of the year. This was for two reasons. WEC operates in one of the most balanced regulatory environments for a utility in Wisconsin combined with a stable service area which led us to add the name early in the quarter. Additionally, the Fund is overweight equities, and this provides a quasi-fixed income holding in Utilities where a utility has a limited return profile within its regulatorily defined returns.

The Fund also started a new position in Amazon in the second quarter, where the company is well positioned to continue capturing market share in retail while also growing its market-leading cloud business. The Fund took advantage of weakness in the stock during April to start the position as tariff news and a precipitous market decline provided an opportunity to build a position.

Alphabet led underperformance in the Communications sector during the first half of the year as there is increasing concern about the impact of generative AI on Alphabet’s search business and whether it will be able to meaningfully respond. We have been trimming the Fund’s holdings in Alphabet as part of the transition to Amazon but also to reflect lower confidence in the company’s ability to maintain its competitive advantage longer term.

Since the beginning of the year, looking through the volatility and shifting narratives, short-term interest rates have fallen while 20-30 year interest rates have largely stood still. This has benefited the Fund as there are very few longer-term securities held in the Fund. Credit spreads, which were reflective of a benign credit environment at the beginning of the year, expanded with the market selloff in April but quickly retreated at the end of June. We have found fewer opportunities to opportunistically find excess spread lately and have generally been raising credit quality in the portfolio in small increments. We will continue to execute our strategy in fixed income, whereby we seek pockets of value and selectively research and buy bonds to help us achieve long-term outperformance.

Our equity style leads us to select companies with durable competitive advantages and attractive long-term growth prospects as this generally leads to sustainable investment returns. We seek to enter and build positions in companies at reasonable valuations. With the recent equity gyrations, the opportunities to build positions at attractive valuations have been improving, where we recently did this with Amazon. Additionally, new layers of analysis are required with the shifting geopolitical dynamics. Notably, as it relates to this, we have long had a general preference for domestic equities and domestic fixed income, which we believe leaves us less exposed to some emerging risks. While we do not make big calls on interest rates or credit cycles, we have increasingly positioned the fixed income to be less volatile in a credit cycle and continually work to improve the portfolio for the long run. This should allow us to take advantage of dislocations in the marketplace should they occur. We believe our consistent investment approach to both stocks and bonds will deliver sustainable performance over the long haul.

data.title: Mairs & Power Growth Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Overview

The stock market entered bear territory in early April (20% below its peak), only to rise 25% from that low by the end of the quarter, exemplifying an unsettled environment. Tariff uncertainty, conflicts in the Middle East, rising national debt, and the worst start to a year for the dollar since 1973, have created volatility that led the markets right back to where they started at the beginning of the year.

Looking at the indices for the second quarter, the S&P 500 Total Return (TR) was 10.94% for the quarter and is 6.20% for the year. Meanwhile, the Russell 2000 (TR) was 8.50% in the quarter and is -1.79% year-to-date, and the Bloomberg U.S. Government/Credit Bond Index was 1.22% in the second quarter and is 3.95% so far in 2025. Growth stocks once again took a leadership position, particularly in Information Technology and Communication Services, while Energy and Health Care lagged. In all, nine of 11 sectors finished the quarter in positive territory.

Despite ongoing hectic headlines, the economic backdrop has remained relatively stable. The pace of inflation continues to decelerate, with the Consumer Price Index (CPI) rising just 2.4% year-over-year in June, down from 3.1% in February. Additionally, the ISM manufacturing and services surveys both improved in recent months, though manufacturing remains in contractionary territory. On the consumer front, the University of Michigan’s Index of Consumer Sentiment rebounded for the first time in six months as expectations improved around tariffs and inflation.

On the negative side, the job market is showing some softening. As of the June ADP National Employment Report, private sector employment declined by 33,000 jobs, marking the first monthly drop in more than two years. While the Labor Department’s employment report showed unexpectedly positive job growth and a lower unemployment rate, nearly half of the increases in nonfarm payrolls came from the government sector, suggesting private employers are pulling back on hiring due to an ongoing uncertain environment.

After contracting in the first quarter of the year, equity market valuation levels expanded back into above-average territory during the second quarter. Currently, the forward 12-month price-to-earnings ratio for the S&P 500 is 22 times earnings, above its five-and- 10-year average. However, as has been the case for a while now, the S&P 600 Small Cap Index P/E is 15 times earnings, which is below both its 5-and-10-year average, indicating a persistent undervaluation of smaller companies in the current environment.

Future Outlook

The mixed economic data has led the Federal Reserve (Fed) to maintain its wait- and-see approach, holding interest rates steady as it monitors the potential impact from tariffs, which remain a wild card. Yet, with inflation pressure easing and the labor market cooling, the likelihood of additional Fed rate cuts in the second half of the year appears to be increasing.

A key support for the recent stock market rally has been the resiliency of corporate earnings, which have been remarkably stable despite the unsettled macro environment. Analysts expect 9% growth for the full year, a reduction from the 13% projected at the beginning of 2025 but an indication that companies are navigating the volatile environment with minimal disruption so far. However, trade uncertainty is likely to remain, and we expect more twists and turns ahead.

Beyond tariffs, inflation, and interest rates, the fervor around artificial intelligence came roaring back in the second quarter. We expect this to continue as the technology and investment surrounding AI, automation, and robotics are moving at breakneck speed. We’re seeing impacts and upgrades within a year, if not a few months.

We believe these technological innovations promise to reshape not just markets, but the way businesses operate. We also believe these are the early days of disruption to entire industries, companies, and the economy. Regardless of how far along those innovations are and what’s yet to come, the pace of technological advancement in recent months is nothing short of extraordinary. We remain positive on the long-term prospects for AI, automation, and robotics. From logistics to healthcare to software development, companies are already realizing gains in productivity and efficiency. Still, we are focused on our valuation discipline to find opportunities in the market. We continue to seek companies with durable competitive advantages and especially those with the scale and wisdom to sacrifice short-term profits for long-term investments in the technological advancements needed to stay ahead of their competitors.

Performance Review

The Mairs & Power Growth Fund is off to a slow start to the year, up just 1.98% year-to- date and trailing both the S&P 500 Total Return (TR), which is up 6.20%, as well as its peer group as measured by the Morningstar Large Blend index which is up 5.72% over the same period of time.

Performance data quoted represents past performance and does not guarantee future results. The investment return and principal value of an investment will fluctuate so that an investor’s shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance of the Fund may be lower or higher than the performance quoted. For the most recent month-end performance figures, please call Shareholder Services at (800) 304-7404. Expense Ratio 0.62%.

The first half of the year proved to be a rollercoaster ride for stocks. Markets were initially shaken by tariff threats but rebounded swiftly once many of the trade policy measures were postponed, giving countries additional time to negotiate deals with the United States. This delay also provided an opportunity for many of our portfolio companies to proactively implement price increases, attempting to mitigate the impact of rising input costs. An important focus during our durable competitive advantage (DCA) research process is to ensure that our holdings possess the pricing power necessary to effectively pass cost inflation onto their customer base. As such, we believe tariffs will have a limited impact on our holdings’ long-term performance.

The Fund’s underperformance relative to its benchmark can be attributed almost entirely to stock selection. Year-to-date, the S&P 500’s performance has remained narrowly driven by a select group of mega-cap stocks, notably Meta, NVIDIA (NVDA), Microsoft (MSFT), Broadcom (AVGO), Netflix (NFLX), and JPMorgan & Chase (JPM). While we own many of these names, we do not own all of these names, which has hurt relative performance to a certain extent.

The Fund’s underperformance was further impacted by weakness in several of our larger, long-term holdings, particularly UnitedHealth Group, which faced a challenging start to the year. The stock declined nearly 50% from recent highs after missing earnings expectations for the first time in almost two decades and cutting its 2025 guidance, which was closely followed by the sudden resignation of its CEO. However, we believe many of the company’s current problems appear fixable, even if they will take time to rectify. In addition, former CEO Stephen Hemsley has returned to lead the company, having previously driven impressive growth and expansion during nearly a decade at the helm, including the successful buildout of Optum Health. We view Hemsley’s return as a highly positive development and expect he will bring renewed focus and vigor to the organization. Accordingly, we added to our position in the company.

Microsoft has become the Fund’s largest holding, now accounting for more than 12% of assets. The stock is up 20% year-to-date and more than five-fold since our initial investment back in 2018. We remain comfortable with this outsized weighting for three primary reasons: (1) Microsoft’s extensive and diverse customer base broadly reflects the overall economy, (2) the company possesses a durable and significant competitive advantage, and (3) we believe Microsoft is uniquely positioned to benefit from the widespread adoption of AI due to its substantial investments in AI technology and an entrenched customer base already leveraging its Office suite. True, the stock is no longer as cheap as it once was, but we see considerable growth opportunities ahead.

Continuing that theme, AI remains front-and-center for investors. We recognized early on that AI would profoundly reshape the economy and society, but the speed of adoption has still surprised us. For perspective, it took nearly 16 years for cell phones and seven years for the internet to reach 100 million users. Meanwhile, ChatGPT, a generative AI chatbot utilizing large language models (LLMs), reached that milestone in just two months. From enablers of AI, such as NVIDIA, Microsoft, Amazon (AMZN), and WEC Energy (WEC), to application providers like Salesforce (CRM) and Microsoft, and finally to early adopters, including UnitedHealth Group, Roche (OTCQX:RHHBY), and JPMorgan & Chase, we firmly believe the Fund is well-positioned to capitalize on the rapid advancement of AI.

Case in point is JPMorgan, one of the Fund’s strongest performers, which is up more than 20% year-to-date. Key drivers include robust growth in investment banking, anticipation of lower interest rates, and a more favorable regulatory environment. Additionally, we are particularly enthusiastic about the company’s strategic use of AI. JPMorgan’s proprietary “LLM Suite,” is actively utilized by approximately 200,000 employees for tasks such as drafting memos, document review, and preparing client materials. Within wealth management, its “Connect Coach” platform has boosted advisor productivity through real-time client data integration. In commercial and investment banking, approximately 175 AI use cases have been deployed, notably reducing client onboarding costs by 40% and streamlining payment processes through predictive modeling.

While Technology stocks roared back in the second quarter, a pullback in the sector earlier in the year provided an opportunity to initiate a position in Meta. Meta owns and operates several prominent social media platforms, including Facebook, Instagram, and WhatsApp, each maintaining significant global user bases with billions of active users. While user growth at Facebook has moderated, Instagram continues to expand steadily, and WhatsApp has emerged as a crucial communication and commerce tool utilized by over 200 million businesses worldwide. Additionally, the company is actively leveraging AI to boost user engagement and help marketers more effectively manage advertising campaigns across its platforms.

The Fund also initiated a small position in IDACORP during the quarter. Based in Boise, Idaho, IDACORP is a regulated electric utility serving customers in southern Idaho and eastern Oregon. Among Utilities, we view IDACORP as having one of the sector’s strongest growth profiles. That’s because Idaho is experiencing robust population growth driven by a favorable cost-of-living environment and plentiful employment opportunities. Additionally, commercial customers are expected to significantly boost electric demand, exemplified by Micron’s construction of a $15 billion memory fab plant, and Meta’s new data center, which is expected to require 325 megawatts of continuous electric demand. Given these favorable dynamics, we believe IDACORP is well-positioned to deliver consistent earnings growth, and attractive shareholder returns in the years ahead.

Finally, the Fund initiated a position in Medpace Holdings, a leading full-service contract research organization focused on Phase I-IV clinical trials and serving small and mid-sized biotech, pharmaceutical, and medical device companies. The company designs, organizes, and manages clinical trials for its customers, who often lack the internal infrastructure to do it themselves. By focusing on smaller customers, Medpace has indexed itself to the fastest growing and most profitable portion of the market. We believe the company has an advantage over its peers due to its full service offering, unique go-to-market, and therapeutic expertise that it has built up organically over three decades. The stock has underperformed over the past year largely due to concerns over biotech funding, which we believe created an attractive entry point into a quality company.

Despite a challenging first half of 2025, we remain firmly committed to the disciplined investment strategy that has rewarded our shareholders since the inception of the Mairs & Power Growth Fund in 1958. While staying true to our investment philosophy and strict value discipline, we have diversified into Technology stocks, which have been favorable for our investors. Amongst other things, AI is dismantling barriers previously thought insurmountable, particularly within robotics. Recent breakthroughs in Vision-Language-Action models are empowering robots to interpret natural language instructions alongside visual information, significantly expanding their applications. In short, the AI era is already here, and we believe the robotics era will be quick to follow. As always, with a steadfast focus on long-term value creation, we are carefully evaluating investment opportunities within robotics, ensuring alignment with our established investment principles.

data.title: Provident Financial Services: 3 Reasons Why I Wouldn't Buy This Bank
data.description:
Provident Financial Services (NYSE:PFS) is a bank founded way back in 1839 and based in Jersey City, New Jersey. Its dividend yield is currently quite high at 5.39%, and investors are wondering how attractive it really is.

As we will see in this article, the sustainability of the dividend does not seem to be a problem today, but my rating is still not a buy. Personally, I do not see the potential for a satisfactory capital gain, and above all, there are three reasons that lead me to avoid it.

The three reasons that lead me to avoid it

Before discussing the three reasons that lead me to avoid it, I would like to point out that PFS is a solid bank overall, and my intention is not to cause alarm. There are simply certain characteristics that lead me to avoid it as an investment, but that does not mean that it does not have positive aspects.

For example, the net interest margin is steadily rising, a sign that the company is navigating well in a rather complex macroeconomic environment. Interest rates have remained unchanged, but expectations about rates are constantly changing, yet PFS has managed to improve its profitability.

At the same time, its tangible book value per share, the most important component in a bank's valuation, continues to rise gradually. Clearly, unrealized losses on AFS securities are still weighing heavily, but the Fed seems determined to cut rates in September, which may give bond yields some breathing room.

That said, here are the three factors that lead me to avoid this bank.

CRE concentration ratio off the norm

The CRE concentration ratio is composed of CRE loans in the numerator and total capital in the denominator; the higher it is, the more the bank is exposed to CRE loans. As the latter are rather cyclical (and therefore risky), supervisory authorities suggest keeping it below 300%, but banks can go further if they wish. Well, PFS has gone further, much further.

It currently stands at 443%, a level that I consider far too risky. Although there have been improvements compared to last year, we are still a long way from 300%, which is already very high. This means that the bank is heavily exposed to CRE loans and could be hit hard in the event of a recession.

Personally, I prefer banks that are less cyclical than average, and PFS is the exact opposite. Clearly, the reverse is also true. When the economy is strong, cyclical businesses can deliver exciting performances, but doubts arise after the recent labor market report revision. The Fed will almost certainly cut rates in September as the labor market is creaking, and this does not seem to me to be the ideal macro scenario for such a cyclical bank.

Loan-to-deposit ratio too high

Personally, I prefer banks with a loan-to-deposit ratio below 90%, as this gives them room for maneuver should they want to take advantage of certain opportunities. PFS's loan-to-deposit ratio is 102%, meaning that loans exceed total deposits.

As a result, its balance sheet appears rather rigid and not very receptive to change. With not enough deposits and being fully invested, it is difficult to further increase the loan portfolio.

Compared to the previous quarter, there was an increase of 1.70%, a rather low growth rate that is unlikely to reach mid-single digits: the deposit base is not large enough to finance origination. A bank with an inflexible balance sheet is bound to break when times get tough.

Furthermore, still focusing on assets, non-performing loans are gradually increasing in relation to total loans. This is not an alarming situation, but it is not positive either. Basically, loans are growing slowly, and those already on the balance sheet are showing signs of weakness.

Deposit composition is deteriorating

The bank's rigidity stems from deposits that are struggling to improve.

The results for the last quarter were not encouraging, as the composition of deposits took a step backwards. Not only did non-interest bearing deposits fall by 0.60%, but wholesale deposits (including brokered deposits) rose by 80.40%. The latter are rather expensive and point to PFS's inability to raise capital at low cost.

If the bank were able to attract more depositors, the benefits would also be reflected in the loan-to-deposit ratio. Deposits are the lifeblood of banks, and without them they are stuck.

Conclusion

Despite these three aspects, PFS remains a solid bank, but it must improve its deposits. With them it could finance new loans, increase its net interest income, and ultimately, its tangible book value.

The bank currently offers a very attractive dividend yield of 5.39%, which seems quite sustainable

EPS covers it amply, but take a look at dividend growth: the 5Y CAGR was 0.85%. In other words, the dividend is sustainable because it has remained unchanged. If it had increased, EPS would have barely covered it.

If you invest in this bank, you could get your 5.39% gross return over the next few years, but personally, I avoid dividend-paying companies if the dividend is not increased over time. For me, the dividend yield on cost is the most important thing, and I doubt that PFS can guarantee that.

Finally, the stock does not even look cheap at the moment.

Its price to tangible book value is almost perfectly in line with the 3Y average, which is why I believe the stock is fairly valued. I don't see much upside at the current price, just a high but flat dividend. However, is it really worth it?

The surprising labor market data has cast doubt on the true strength of the US economy, and at this stage I would avoid overly cyclical businesses regardless: PFS is definitely one of these and does not even appear to be trading at a discount.

data.title: Differentiation Matters As Moat Stocks Lead In July
data.description:
In a market dominated by mega-cap momentum, July showcased the strength of the Moat Index’s stock selection and structural discipline.

In July, U.S. equity markets continued their summer advance, marking the third straight month of gains as the S&P 500 rose 2.2% and set a fresh new record high. The Nasdaq Composite gained 3.7%, while the Dow Jones Industrial Average ended the month largely flat. Investor sentiment was bolstered by strong corporate earnings, positive GDP data, and the passage of the One Big Beautiful Bill, which enacted new tax legislation extending previous cuts. However, renewed tariff discussions with multiple countries and a softer-than-expected jobs report toward month's end introduced volatility. The Technology sector remained at the forefront, propelled by AI developments, even as the Federal Reserve once again kept interest rates unchanged. Continued large downward revisions in jobs data did, however, boost market expectations for possible rate reductions later in the year.

The Morningstar Wide Moat Focus Index (the “Moat Index”) gained more than 3% in July, outpacing both the broad market S&P 500 as well as the equal-weighted variant. The outperformance came despite the Moat Index’s structural underweight to top mega-cap technology names. Strong stock selection during the month, particularly within the consumer staples and industrial sectors, more than offset sector allocations. The strategy continues to provide differentiated exposure, which has become increasingly difficult for investors to find amid historical levels of concentration in the U.S. equity markets.

Smaller stocks also saw advances during the month, but like much of the year so far, the cohort’s performance lagged relative to large caps. The Morningstar US Small-Mid Cap Moat Focus Index (the “SMID Moat Index”) posted a 1.9% gain in July, outpacing both the broad small- and mid-cap benchmarks which returned 0.9% and 1.6%, respectively. Despite their muted performance, many are keeping a close eye on small and mid caps as the segment’s prospects could improve with a dovish pivot by the Fed.

Moat Stocks Lead the Pack in July

Moat Index July Highlights: Chips and Ships Steer Gains

In July, the Moat Index benefited from strong stock selection, as several companies delivered solid earnings results that prompted the market to revalue their shares more in line with Morningstar’s fair value estimates. This earnings-driven rerating more than offset headwinds from the Index’s equal-weighted construction in the current mega-cap driven market. As a result, the Index outpaced the broader, tech-heavy market while continuing to provide differentiated exposure.

Wide-moat semiconductor testing leader Teradyne (TER) was the top contributor to the Moat Index in July, with shares rising nearly 20% following second-quarter earnings and positive forward commentary. While sales declined year over year, management’s guidance pointed to a return to growth in the second half of 2025, and upbeat messaging around demand for AI chip testing and robotics drove a sharp rerating. Morningstar believes Teradyne is well positioned to benefit from growing complexity in semiconductors, particularly in high-bandwidth memory and custom AI accelerators, and sees a recent robotics design win as a meaningful growth driver heading into 2026. With its leading market share, sticky customer relationships, and a robust R&D pipeline, Morningstar maintains a $115 fair value estimate, suggesting there may still be additional upside ahead.

Defense contractor Huntington Ingalls (HII) was also a key contributor to Moat Index performance in July, with shares gaining on the back of strong quarterly results and guidance. While quarterly profit declined due to temporary shipbuilding delays, revenue rose modestly, and management highlighted the arrival of key components that should allow construction to accelerate in the second half of the year. Morningstar expects gradual margin improvement over the coming years, driven by more efficient throughput and higher-margin contracts, particularly in submarine programs. With the U.S. Navy continuing to invest in shipyard capacity and submarine production, Morningstar sees durable long-term demand for Huntington Ingalls’ uniquely positioned shipbuilding operations. Following the update, Morningstar raised its fair value estimate to $324 per share, suggesting shares remain attractively priced.

Other top contributors within the Moat Index during the month include global prestige beauty market leader, Estee Lauder (EL), security products and solutions company, Allegion (ALLE), as well as semiconductor design software provider, Synopsys (SNPS).

Companies detracting the most in July came from a mix of sectors, with industrials accounting for two of the five names on the list: United Parcel Service (UPS) and fluidics equipment manufacturer IDEX Corp. (IEX). Other notable detractors included fixed-income trading platform MarketAxess (MKTX), software and analytics giant Adobe Inc. (ADBE), and enterprise cloud leader Salesforce Inc. (CRM).

SMID Moat Index July Highlights: Travel Strength and M&A Activity Shine

The SMID Moat Index outpaced both the small-cap and mid-cap benchmarks in July, driven by strong stock selection across several parts of the portfolio. Industrials and consumer discretionary were standout areas, with four of the month’s top five contributors coming from these two sectors.

Norwegian Cruise Line Holdings (NCLH) topped the SMID Moat Index in July, marking its second consecutive month as a leading contributor. Shares rallied 26% as investor confidence grew around the company’s consistent pricing strength, robust travel demand, and expanding onboard spend. Morningstar believes Norwegian remains well positioned to deliver improving returns on invested capital, supported by efficient scale, cost advantages and brand loyalty across its portfolio. The company’s younger fleet and pipeline of new ships provide ongoing pricing power, while cost initiatives and scale benefits continue to support margin expansion. Morningstar maintains its fair value estimate of $31.50 per share, suggesting shares may still have room to run.

Chart Industries (GTLS), a provider of cryogenic equipment and specialty solutions used across LNG, hydrogen, and industrial gas markets, was also a top contributor to SMID Moat Index performance in July, with shares jumping 20% following news that the company had entered into a definitive agreement to be acquired by Baker Hughes. The all-cash deal, valued at $13.6 billion or $210 per share, marked a premium over Chart’s prior trading levels and sent the stock sharply higher. Morningstar views the offer price as aligned with its fair value estimate and sees the acquisition as a positive outcome for Chart shareholders, offering greater certainty than a prior bid from Flowserve. Despite softer second-quarter sales, the company’s aftermarket service business remains a key strength, and Morningstar continues to believe Chart’s specialty products and repair-driven revenue model support its economic moat.

Companies detracting the most in July within the SMID Moat Index included two names from the auto industry, with auto retailers Lithia Motors (LAD) and CarMax (KMX) weighing on performance during the month. Other notable laggards were toy manufacturer Mattel (MAT), health care supplier Baxter International (BAX), and communications provider Charter Communications (CHTR).

data.title: British American Tobacco: After A Double This Stock Is Fully Valued And Ripe For A Pullback (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
I wrote a bullish article on British American Tobacco (NYSE:BTI) in May 2024, and at that time, the stock was trading for just $30 per share and it was yielding 9.5%. My article suggested that the window could be closing for those wanting to lock in a 9.5% yield and that turned out to be true. Since my article was published, this stock has provided stellar total returns of about 106%, while the S&P 500 Index (SPY) has generated a total return of just under 20% in the same time period. At the time I wrote the article, I felt the stock was too undervalued and the yield was so generous, but after these huge gains, I think it is time to lock in the amazing gains and allocate the proceeds to new investment opportunities and even to money market funds. Let's take a closer look at why I think it is time to sell:

The Chart

By looking at the chart below, you might think this is a fast growing tech stock as it has been on a huge bull run for the past year. Most of the gains have come in 2025, and the stock is now overbought and trading well above key support levels. The 50-day moving average is around $49 and the 200-day moving is just about $40. When I look at this chart, I see a stock that looks overly extended and potentially ripe for a meaningful pullback. With the stock now trading above $56, a pullback to the 50-day moving average would represent a decline of about 10%. A more meaningful correction back to the 200-day moving average would represent a decline of around 30%. I view a decline of this magnitude (30%) as less likely, but I do see at least a 10% pullback as very likely.

Earnings Estimates And The Balance Sheet

The estimates provided by Seeking Alpha below show that this stock is now trading for about 12 times earnings. I believe that given the lack of meaningful growth for the tobacco industry, this represents full valuation if not overvaluation for this stock. As you can see from the revenue estimates below, this company is only expected to grow the top line by about 2% in 2026 and around 3% for 2027. This is not the type of growth that I want to see for a stock that is trading at 12 times earnings.

As for the balance sheet, this company has about $48.49 billion in debt, and $6.72 billion in cash. This shows that there is a significant amount of debt and higher debt levels generally increase risk levels for shareholders. Higher debt levels also typically limit what investors will pay for a stock as well, and this is one more factor that leads me to believe that this stock is fully valued, if not overvalued right now.

The Dividend Is No Longer Attractive Enough For Me

British American Tobacco currently offers a quarterly dividend of $0.75 per share, which yields around 5.5%. In my last article, I saw the dividend yield at 9.5% as being a very attractive investment to lock in while investors were so negative and treating this yield as being unsustainable. It turned out the yield was not sustainable, but not because of a dividend cut, but rather because the stock has just about doubled in value. Because of the rapid share price appreciation, this stock is only yielding around 5.5%, and I don't feel this yield is adequate to compensate for some of the potential risk factors that come with this company and the tobacco industry which includes regulatory risks, high debt levels, and very slow growth, just to name a few.

I also believe a yield of around 5.5% is not attractive enough when compared to other income investments. The average money market fund now yields around 4.2% and I see that as a better overall place to park my money, given the risk to reward I would get from British American Tobacco shares which yield just around 1.3% more than a money market fund, but could be prone to a major pullback of 10% or more.

Here Are My Reasons For Selling

1) The yield no longer is attractive compared to other lower risk income investments.

2) This stock appears overbought, and potentially overvalued, as it now trades well above key support levels. I believe this stock is ripe for a major pullback.

3) In contrast to how negatively many investors and analysts were when I wrote my last article, I now see what could be too much bullishness and this could be the sign of a top. A recent article from Seeking Alpha states that some analysts are getting more bullish on this stock, even after the massive run it has had in 2025. Let's remember, this stock has had total returns of over 100% since my last article in 2024, and this company is only growing revenue in the very low single digits.

4) I believe this is an opportune time to sell into strength. People talk about buying opportunities all the time, but we also need to consider selling opportunities and that is what I see now.

Here Is My Potential Strategy Going Forward

I've sold my position, and I am happy to deploy the proceeds into other income investments. Money market funds are still an attractive place to park cash and considering I see a pullback of 10% or more as likely for this stock, I will happily accept a slightly lower yield in exchange for a big reduction in risk. However, I would consider getting back into this stock if there is a pullback of at least 10%, which would take it back to the 50-day moving average. But, I would only buy a partial position at that level which is around $49, and I would increase my buying, if the stock gets closer to the 200-day moving average. I certainly would not be buying this stock at this time.

Risk Factors And Where I Could Be Wrong

After a very weak jobs report for July, and major downward revisions for May and June, it appears that the Fed will probably lower interest rates in September and that could boost dividend stocks. However, I think this news is already priced into the stock, and it could even be a sell on the news event whereby this stock could even decline in September after a rate cut. But I could be wrong, and this stock definitely has momentum, so that could continue and send the shares even higher. Even though I have valuation concerns, I do believe that management is doing a good job and the company has been posting solid results, which could continue, especially if margins can be expanded and price increases continue. If this company is able to drive strong results in terms of profit margins, it could also keep the stock at elevated levels or push it higher.

In Summary

I feel that this stock has topped out for now, and the easy money has probably been made after doubling since my last article. The sentiment for this stock has gone from being negative to what I feel is overly bullish now given the limited growth potential for the tobacco industry and the ongoing regulatory risks. Given all of this, and because of the huge rally, the yield is no longer enough for me. I believe this is a potential top and therefore a selling opportunity. I also believe a better buying opportunity will likely come in the future with a pullback of at least 10%.

data.title: U.S. Health Insurers See Costs Mount, Income Estimates Decline In Q2
data.description:
Many of the largest publicly traded US health insurers had a difficult second quarter as rising costs associated with government-subsidized health plans exacerbated ongoing declines in earnings per share and income.

Of the six largest publicly traded US managed care insurers, all but Humana Inc. (HUM) released operating income figures that fell below analyst consensus estimates.

A key factor behind this trend was rising medical costs, which resulted in decreased revenues and lower-than-anticipated EPS.

Humana outperforms peers

Humana, which raised its full-year earnings projections, bucked the trends that have dragged down the broader managed care sector. The company's operating income exceeded analyst consensus estimates by 6.1% from the start of the year through August, according to an analysis by S&P Global Market Intelligence.

"While we still have challenges to navigate, the external environment this year continues to evolve largely in line with our expectations, and we are executing against our plan," President and CEO James Rechtin said in a July 30 earnings call.

The quarter's results were buoyed by growth in Humana's Medicaid and pharmacy business, CenterWell, Rechtin said, as well as a smaller-than-anticipated decline in individual membership among Medicare plans.

Medicare Advantage, expanded versions of the government-subsidized plans offered via a partnership with private insurers, has been an issue for Humana in recent years as a post-COVID increase in utilization led to higher costs. Trends were in line with expectations during the second quarter, according to CFO Celeste Mellet, and there was no apparent escalation.

The sharpest miss in operating income from the consensus estimate during the same period was from Centene Corp. (CNC), which fell 93.2% below analyst estimates.

Centene dropped its earnings guidance significantly to adjusted diluted EPS of $1.75 from an adjusted diluted EPS of greater than $7.25 for the year. The health insurer withdrew its original guidance in July, in the face of significantly higher aggregate market morbidity compared to the assumptions underpinning the guidance in its marketplace business.

In addition to the significant drop in EPS and consensus operating income, Centene is also projected to have the highest medical loss ratio in 2025, topping out at around 91.8%.

Ongoing woes

Managed care companies such as UnitedHealth Group Inc. (UNH) have faced various issues throughout 2025, including high costs associated with the senior-aimed Medicare plans that have plagued the managed care sector, as well as ongoing federal efforts to cut funding for low-income-focused Medicaid plans.Amid these and other challenges, Andrew Witty stepped down as CEO of UnitedHealth Group in May following an "unusual and unacceptable" first quarter and was immediately succeeded by Stephen Hemsley, the company's chairman. The second quarter saw these woes continue, with UnitedHealth's consensus operating income falling 43.2% from January to August.

Much of the blame for the poor quarter in the sector was laid at the feet of Medicaid rates, which are inadequate to address shifting morbidity trends, Centene CEO Sarah London said in a July 25 earnings call.

The shift in morbidity trends, which were as much as 16% or 17% in some states, meant the business was underpriced, London said. As of the morning of July 25, the company had filed for 2026 price increases in 17 states and expected to submit filings in up to 12 additional states in the next week, she said. Centene expects state certification of the new rates over the next month.

"Based on what we know today, we continue to believe that we will be able to reprice the 2026 portfolio to account for a substantial majority of our Marketplace membership, and our goal is to reprice 100% of the book," London said.

EPS on the decline

Four of the six largest publicly traded US managed care insurers forecast adjusted diluted EPS estimates lower than in the previous quarter.

Elevance Health Inc.'s (ELV) EPS estimate remains the largest of the group, sitting at $30 a share, just 4 cents lower than the consensus estimate and down from $34.50 prior to the second-quarter earnings release.

While this adjustment may be disappointing to some shareholders, President and CEO Gail Boudreaux said during a July 17 earnings call that it reflects the same pressures that others in the sector have now confirmed, particularly elevated medical cost trends across the Affordable Care Act and slower-than-expected Medicaid rate alignment.

"Importantly, this decision is anchored in our view that the elevated trends we are now observing will persist and reflects our updated visibility into the second half of the year," Boudreaux said. "It is not based on assumptions of a near-term recovery."

The Cigna Group (CI) was the only managed care insurer to maintain its diluted estimate, at $29.60 a share.

Molina Healthcare Inc. (MOH), which lowered its EPS forecast from $22 a share to $19 a share, believes it has reached a "floor," President and CEO Joseph Zubretsky said in an earnings call.

Molina believes that the cost trend could moderate "from this conservative indication and produce earnings upside," Zubretsky said.

"Even in this broadly challenging environment, we have the confidence and clarity to provide a specific earnings-per-share guidance floor with upside potential," the CEO said. "We continue to grow premium this year at 9% and 19% over the past few years."
